validating.definition: Showing someone that their feelings are real and important.
validating.example: Validating a child's feelings helps them feel heard.

bearing.definition: An effect or influence on something.
bearing.example: How you manage feelings has a bearing on your child.

instincts.definition: Natural feelings that tell you what to do without thinking.
instincts.example: Trust your instincts when something feels wrong.

deny.definition: To say something is not true or not important.
deny.example: If we deny how a child feels, we dull their instincts.

dulling.definition: Making something less sharp or sensitive.
dulling.example: Denying feelings risks dulling a child's natural instincts.

excellent.definition: Very good; of very high quality.
excellent.example: It is an excellent book about talking to children.

pretend.definition: To act as if something is true when it is not.
pretend.example: The older boy wanted to pretend to be a dog.

icky.example: It made her feel icky, so she left the pool.

react.definition: To act in response to something that happened.
react.example: Her friends had been trained not to react to certain things.

dismiss.definition: To treat something as not important.
dismiss.example: It is too easy to dismiss a child's fears.

hack.definition: A quick, clever trick or shortcut.
hack.example: If there is one big hack, it is this: do not fight about feelings.

battle.example: Do not get into a battle about what a child is feeling.

agenda.definition: A list of things you need to do; your plan.
agenda.example: You have your own agenda to worry about.

dialogue.example: Saying 'You hate school right now' opens up the dialogue.

shutting.definition: Closing something, stopping it from continuing.
shutting.example: Saying 'that's that' shuts down the conversation.

grab.definition: To take something quickly and roughly.
grab.example: We grab a toddler to put their coat on.

toddler.definition: A very young child who has just started walking.
toddler.example: The toddler refused to put on her coat.

observing.example: Start by observing and listening to your child.

reflecting.definition: Thinking about something carefully; also, repeating back what someone said.
reflecting.example: Reflecting their feelings back to them shows you understand.

pace.definition: The speed at which someone does something.
pace.example: Respect your child's slower pace in the morning.

catastrophe.example: What seems small to you can feel like a catastrophe to a toddler.

resilience.definition: The ability to recover quickly from difficult situations.
resilience.example: She worried her son would never build up resilience.

floods.definition: A very large amount, like water rushing in.
floods.example: Before, he would cry in floods of tears.

imperceptibly.definition: So slowly that you cannot notice it happening.
imperceptibly.example: The change happened gradually and imperceptibly.

soothing.definition: Making someone feel calm and less upset.
soothing.example: Keep soothing your child — it works over time.

expedient.definition: The quickest and most practical way to do something.
expedient.example: Accepting feelings is actually the most expedient path.

philosophy.definition: A belief or way of thinking about something.
philosophy.example: Stick with the philosophy of soothing the tears.

internalize.definition: To make something part of yourself; to absorb it.
internalize.example: Over time, children internalize the soothing their parents give them.

revert.definition: To go back to an old habit or way of doing things.
revert.example: It is easy to revert to how your parents treated you.

disapproved.definition: Showed that you think something is wrong or bad.
disapproved.example: She was disapproved of for having inconvenient feelings.

apologizing.example: You may find yourself apologizing for crying.

leap.definition: A big jump; here, a big, brave step.
leap.example: Accepting feelings can feel like taking a leap into the unknown.

ancestral.definition: Coming from your family in the past, going back many generations.
ancestral.example: You are breaking links in your ancestral emotional chain.

chain.definition: A series of connected things; here, patterns passed from parent to child.
chain.example: Break the chain of denying feelings.

slip-ups.example: Slip-ups are normal and will not ruin your child forever.

adequate.example: You must be an adequate container for your child's emotions.

practise.definition: To do something regularly to get better at it.
practise.example: Practise dealing with your own emotions.

define.definition: To explain what something means; to describe clearly.
define.example: Define the feeling rather than defining yourself by it.

identifying.definition: Seeing yourself as the same as something.
identifying.example: Say 'I feel sad' instead of identifying with sadness.

mature.definition: To grow and develop; to become more adult.
mature.example: As children mature, they can use words for their feelings.

logical.definition: Based on reason and thinking, not feelings.
logical.example: The logical part of the brain becomes stronger with age.

dominant.definition: Stronger or more powerful than others.
dominant.example: As they grow, the logical part of the brain becomes dominant.

mercy.definition: The power someone has over you; being at the mercy of means being controlled by.
mercy.example: Without words, children are at the mercy of their feelings.

sibling.example: It is normal for a child to have strong feelings about a sibling.

granny.example: The child did not want to visit Granny.

protest.definition: To say or show that you disagree with something.
protest.example: Feeling understood gives them one less thing to protest about.

orchid.definition: A delicate flower; here, a child who is very sensitive to their environment.
orchid.example: An Orchid child needs extra care and attention.

dandelion.definition: A tough wild flower; here, a child who is naturally strong in any environment.
dandelion.example: A Dandelion child handles changes more easily.

ultra-sensitive.definition: Extremely sensitive; affected by things more than most people.
ultra-sensitive.example: Some children are ultra-sensitive to changes around them.

robust.definition: Strong and able to handle difficult conditions.
robust.example: Dandelion children are naturally more robust.

immune.definition: The body's system that protects you from getting sick.
immune.example: Stress can weaken your immune system.

earthquake.definition: When the ground shakes strongly and suddenly.
earthquake.example: The earthquake happened while researchers were studying children.

dismayed.example: The researchers were dismayed because the earthquake changed their study.

stressor.example: The earthquake was an extra stressor for the children.

jeopardize.definition: To put something at risk of being harmed or lost.
jeopardize.example: The earthquake could jeopardize their study results.

capitalize.definition: To take advantage of a situation.
capitalize.example: They decided to capitalize on the earthquake by studying its effects.

crayons.definition: Colored sticks of wax used for drawing.
crayons.example: The children were given crayons and paper to draw.

cheery.example: Some children drew cheery pictures of the disaster.

depicted.definition: Showed or drew something in a picture.
depicted.example: Some children depicted fear and fire in their drawings.

respiratory.definition: Related to breathing and the lungs.
respiratory.example: The happy-drawing group had more respiratory illnesses.

fatalities.definition: Deaths, especially in a disaster or accident.
fatalities.example: Some drawings showed fatalities from the earthquake.

trait.definition: A quality or characteristic that a person has.
trait.example: Expression through art is a human trait.

ownership.definition: Taking control of something as your own.
ownership.example: Art helps us take ownership of the things that scare us.

diminish.example: Each time we express sadness, the sadness diminishes.

essential.example: It is essential that parents listen to their child's feelings.

luxury.definition: Something nice that not everyone can have.
luxury.example: Not many families have the luxury of one parent staying home.

temperament.definition: A person's natural way of thinking and behaving.
temperament.example: Staying home may not suit your temperament.

miserable.example: Children prefer happy parents over miserable ones.

martyred.definition: Suffering in a way that makes others feel guilty.
martyred.example: A child prefers happy parents to martyred ones.

domestic.definition: Related to the home and family life.
domestic.example: Children should be allowed to have feelings about domestic arrangements.

denial.definition: Refusing to accept that something is true.
denial.example: Being in denial about your child's feelings is not helpful.

capacity.definition: The ability to do or hold something.
capacity.example: A child has more capacity for happiness when all feelings are allowed.

convenient.definition: Easy and fitting well into your plans.
convenient.example: Parents want to hear only the convenient feelings.

interpretation.definition: The way you understand or explain something.
interpretation.example: Dr Boyce's interpretation of the study was surprising.

desperately.example: We want our children to be happy so desperately.

trap.definition: A situation that catches you and is hard to get out of.
trap.example: We can fall into the trap of denying our child's feelings.

wisest.definition: Most wise; having the best judgment.
wisest.example: Denial is not the wisest course of action.

stings.definition: Hurts with a sharp, quick pain.
stings.example: Give me a hug while my knee stings.

childminder.definition: A person who is paid to look after children in their home.
childminder.example: The child went to a childminder while her parents worked.
